behaviors.description: The review input is scoped to the lines that changed, not the full content of each modified file
behaviors.justification: A unit test with mocked diff extraction would pass even if the agent reads the full file content later. The real failure is at the orchestration boundary: the agent receives the diff but then ignores it and analyzes the whole file. You need to verify the scoping is enforced across the agent's actual analysis logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test diff showing 5 changed lines in a 1000-line file. Mock the GitHub API to return that diff, then run the agent's analysis. Verify that the agent's findings only reference line numbers within the changed range and never mention content from the unchanged 995 lines. Failure would manifest as the agent discussing code outside the diff scope.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: This requires Integration level because a unit test of diff extraction in isolation would always pass, but integration with the full analysis chain could reveal that the agent ignores the scope constraint.

behaviors.description: Inline comments are attached to the correct positions in the diff view on the pull request
behaviors.justification: The failure is protocol-specific and only visible in GitHub's actual diff view. Line numbering in diffs is non-obvious (context lines, +/- prefixes, header lines), and off-by-one errors or misunderstanding GitHub's line-numbering scheme would only be caught by actual HTTP calls or a strict system-level mock that validates the GitHub API specification.
behaviors.test_description: Post a real comment to a test PR on GitHub (or use a mock that strictly validates GitHub's diff line numbering API spec), specifying a particular line number where the comment should appear. Verify by reading back the PR's diff view (or mock response) that the comment appears at the correct visual position. Failure would be: comment appears 1 line off, or in the wrong file, or with incorrect diff context.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A mock without GitHub's actual diff line numbering rules could hide off-by-one errors; only System level (real API or strict protocol mock) catches this.

behaviors.description: The agent identifies at least one real defect in a pull request that contains a known introduced bug
behaviors.justification: The failure is in the LLM's ability to reason about code and detect bugs. Mocking the analysis always produces mocked results and would never exhibit the real failure: 'analysis ran but the LLM missed the bug.' Only a real LLM call can expose whether the agent's reasoning is sufficient to catch real defects.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test PR with a known, unambiguous bug (e.g., a missing null check before dereferencing, or an off-by-one loop error). Run the agent with a real LLM against this PR. Verify that the agent identifies the bug in its findings. Failure: agent completes but does not mention the introduced defect.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: This requires Agentic level because bug detection depends on the LLM's reasoning; mocking the analysis would hide failures in the LLM's ability to identify defects.

behaviors.description: Every finding listed in the top-level summary corresponds to an inline comment that was also posted — no findings appear in the summary without a matching inline comment
behaviors.justification: This is about orchestration and state consistency. A unit test of summary generation in isolation (with mocked comments) would always show consistency. The real failure is in the coordination logic: the agent generates findings and posts comments, but the logic that ensures every summary item has a matching comment could have bugs. You need integration-level verification that orchestrates both components and verifies the state.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full agent logic with a mock GitHub API that records all posted inline comments. Generate the summary. Verify that every finding mentioned in the summary has a corresponding comment in the recorded list. Failure: summary mentions a bug finding, but no inline comment was posted for it (or vice versa — orphaned comments).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Requires Integration because you must run analysis + comment posting + summary generation together and verify cross-component consistency; unit tests of individual pieces with mocks would mask coordination failures.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — reading the diff, posting inline comments, posting the summary comment — completes correctly for a real pull request without manual intervention
behaviors.justification: This requires orchestrating multiple agentic steps in sequence: (1) fetch and parse diff, (2) LLM analysis of code, (3) post inline comments via API, (4) post summary comment via API. Each step can fail in ways that depend on real I/O, real LLM non-determinism, or network behavior. A single LLM call fails randomly; a sequence of them fails in combinations that only manifest end-to-end.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a real (or System-level mocked) GitHub PR environment with known changes. Run the agent end-to-end against the real PR (or mock with System-level fidelity: real diff parsing, real LLM calls, realistic API responses). Verify that all comments are posted, the summary is posted, and no step in the sequence hangs, errors, or leaves partial state. Failure: agent crashes mid-sequence, posts some comments but not the summary, or times out during LLM analysis.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Workflow level is necessary because individual LLM calls (Agentic) and comment posting (System) succeed, but the coordination of multiple agentic steps in sequence can fail in ways hidden by lower-level tests (e.g., timeout after comment 3 of 5, or LLM inconsistency across calls).
